skill.name: git-workflow
skill.description: Guide Git workflows for commits, branches, merges, rebases, conflict resolution, and team collaboration.
skill.summary: Plan and troubleshoot Git workflows with branch, commit, merge, rebase, and conflict-resolution guidance.
skill.author: supercent-io

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many shell-command, URL, and weak-cryptography patterns. Review found no prompt injection or executable malware, but the skill documents Git commands that can rewrite history, delete branches, clear stashes, and contact remotes. The weak-cryptography alerts are false positives from words like hash, branch names, and documentation text rather than cryptographic code.

medium_findings.title: Potentially Destructive Git Operations
medium_findings.description: The skill documents commands such as force push with lease, branch deletion, stash clearing, and hard reset. These are legitimate Git operations, but they can discard local work, remove recovery points, or rewrite branch history if used without review.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are directly shown in Git workflow examples and include data-loss or history-rewrite operations. The context is educational rather than malicious, so the risk is operational rather than malware-like.

low_findings.title: Shell Command Detections Are Documentation Examples
low_findings.description: Most external-command detections are fenced Markdown or inline Git command examples. They do not execute automatically and are expected for a Git workflow skill, but users may copy commands into a terminal.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are Markdown command snippets and TOON summaries, not executable scripts or code paths. The scanner pattern is real text, but there is no automatic execution mechanism in the skill files.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs Are References Or Example Remote
low_findings.description: The URL at line 478 is an example upstream remote, and lines 521-525 are public Git learning references. No evidence found that these URLs receive secrets or exfiltrate repository data.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URL contexts are visible documentation examples and reference links. There is no network client code, credential handling, webhook, or suspicious endpoint behavior.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Are Keyword False Positives
low_findings.description: The static weak-cryptography detections align with descriptive words such as commit-hash, branch naming examples, JWT in a sample commit message, and GitHub Flow references. No cryptographic algorithm implementation or security-sensitive hash use was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines contain Git vocabulary, branch labels, or documentation text, not calls to MD5, SHA1, DES, or similar weak algorithms. The scanner appears to have matched generic keywords rather than cryptographic code.

dangerous_patterns.title: History Rewrite And Data Loss Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes examples of hard reset, stash clear, branch force deletion, and force push with lease. These patterns should be presented with warnings because they can remove local changes or alter shared history.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The exact Git commands are present and their destructive effects are well-defined. They are not malicious by themselves, but they are risky when copied into a real repository.

content.value_statement: Git workflows can become risky when branches, rebases, and conflicts are handled inconsistently. This skill gives structured guidance for commits, branch updates, merges, rebases, and recovery steps.
actual_capabilities: Explains feature branch creation, naming, and cleanup workflows.
Provides conventional commit message formats and examples.
Guides merge, rebase, conflict resolution, and branch synchronization steps.
Covers stash, cherry-pick, bisect, reflog, and history inspection commands.
Documents Git configuration for user identity, aliases, and editor setup.
Links to Git learning references and workflow models.
limitations: It is documentation only and does not execute Git commands for the user.
Some examples can discard work or rewrite history if copied without review.
It does not inspect the current repository state before suggesting commands.
It does not create pull requests or integrate with hosting provider APIs.

use_cases.title: Standardize Team Git Practices
use_cases.description: Define branch naming, commit structure, and cleanup steps for a development team.

use_cases.title: Resolve Branch Conflicts
use_cases.description: Follow a clear process when a merge or rebase reports conflicting files.

use_cases.title: Recover From Workflow Mistakes
use_cases.description: Use documented reset, revert, reflog, and branch recovery patterns with caution.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Feature Branch
prompt_templates.prompt: I need to start a new feature from main. Suggest a branch name and the Git commands I should run.

prompt_templates.title: Write a Commit Message
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me write a conventional commit message for these changes: [describe the files and behavior changed].

prompt_templates.title: Resolve Rebase Conflicts
prompt_templates.prompt: I rebased my feature branch onto main and Git reported conflicts. Walk me through the safest next steps.

prompt_templates.title: Clean Up Pull Request History
prompt_templates.prompt: I have several messy commits before review. Help me decide whether to squash, reword, or keep them.

output_examples.input: I changed authentication validation and tests. Help with a commit message.
output_examples.output: Suggested commit message: fix(auth): validate login token expiry. Body: update token expiry checks, add tests for expired sessions, and document the expected failure behavior.

output_examples.input: I need to update my feature branch before opening a pull request.
output_examples.output: Recommended flow: check status, commit or stash local work, fetch origin, rebase onto origin/main, resolve conflicts, run tests, then push with lease only if history changed.
Suggested recovery: create a new branch at the current commit, move main back only after confirming the commit was not pushed, then continue work on the new branch.

best_practices: Check repository status and review diffs before staging or resetting files.
Prefer small commits with clear messages that explain the reason for each change.
Treat reset, stash clear, branch deletion, and force push commands as review-required operations.
anti_patterns: Running destructive Git commands before confirming that work is committed, pushed, or backed up.
Force pushing shared branches without checking whether teammates added commits.
Combining unrelated changes into one large commit that is difficult to review or revert.

faq.question: Does this skill run Git commands automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides command examples and workflow guidance that a user may choose to run manually.

faq.question: Why is the security risk rated medium?
faq.answer: The skill is not malicious, but it documents commands that can delete work, rewrite history, or contact remote repositories.

faq.question: Can it help write commit messages?
faq.answer: Yes. It shows conventional commit types and examples for focused commit messages.

faq.question: Can it resolve merge conflicts by itself?
faq.answer: No. It explains the process, but the user must edit conflicted files and verify the result.

faq.question: Does it support team branch workflows?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers feature branches, hotfix branches, rebasing, merging, and cleanup after review.

faq.question: What should users review before following examples?
faq.answer: Users should review current branch, uncommitted changes, remote state, and whether a command rewrites or deletes history.
